summary:
The Voyages of Francis Drake (c. 1567–1596 AD) made the English privateer and navigator one of the most celebrated and feared maritime figures of the Elizabethan age — the second person to circumnavigate the globe (1577–1580), a successful pirate of Spanish Caribbean and Pacific treasure, and a national hero who helped define English Protestant identity in opposition to Catholic Spain. Drake's career epitomized the Elizabethan strategy of 'plausible deniability privateering': raids on Spanish shipping and colonies in the Caribbean and Pacific were technically piracy (conducted without formal letters of marque), but Queen [[event:2471|Elizabeth I]] secretly encouraged and invested in them, receiving a share of the substantial profits while maintaining diplomatic distance.

Drake's circumnavigation (1577–1580), the second in history after [[event:2321|Magellan's]], served multiple purposes: to raid Spanish Pacific ports that had never seen an English ship before (Valparaíso, Callao), to seek the fabled Northwest Passage from the Pacific side, and to demonstrate English oceanic capabilities. Raiding Spanish ports in Chile and Peru, he captured immense treasure — the haul from the capture of the Spanish galleon Cacafuego alone was worth roughly £500,000 (equivalent to perhaps a year's revenues of the English crown). Elizabeth knighted him aboard his ship the Golden Hind on his return. His 'singeing of the King of Spain's beard' at Cádiz (1587), which delayed the Spanish Armada by a year, and his role in the [[event:2339|Armada's]] defeat (1588) further cemented his legendary status. Drake died of dysentery during a final Caribbean expedition in 1596 and was buried at sea off Portobelo, Panama.
